module: Module 8: Antiderivatives and Integration by Substitution

sections.text: AP Calculus AB. Module Eight — antiderivatives and integration by substitution. Last module built Riemann sums and the Fundamental Theorem. Today we turn that into a real toolkit: the basic antiderivative rules, then the single most powerful technique for undoing chain rule — u-substitution. Plus two algebra rescues the AP exam keeps reaching for.

Look at this derivative. The derivative of sine of x-squared is two-x times cosine of x-squared. The chain rule baked the inside derivative right into the answer. Now play the movie backward. If somebody hands you two-x times cosine of x-squared and asks for an antiderivative, you have to spot that two-x as the leftover ghost of a chain rule. That's the whole game today. Integration is detective work.

Three things today. One — antiderivatives, the basic rules, and that little plus-C you can't ever forget. Two — u-substitution, which is just chain rule running backward. Three — two algebra tricks the AP exam loves: long division when the top degree is at least the bottom, and completing the square when you see a tough quadratic in the denominator.

Definition. A function big-F is an antiderivative of little-f if differentiating big-F gives you little-f back. We write the indefinite integral of f-of-x dx equals big-F-of-x plus C. The plus-C matters — when you differentiate, constants vanish, so going backward, any constant could have been there. Forgetting it is one of the most-deducted points on the entire AP exam.

Reverse power rule. The integral of x-to-the-n dx is x-to-the-n-plus-one over n-plus-one, plus C — as long as n is not negative one. Bump the exponent up, divide by the new exponent. The exception is when n equals negative one — one over x — and that integral is the natural log of the absolute value of x, plus C. Constants pull out front, and integrals distribute over sums and differences.

Memorize cold. Integral of sine x dx is negative cosine x plus C. Integral of cosine x dx is sine x plus C — flipped sign, because cosine differentiates to negative sine. Integral of secant-squared x is tangent x. Integral of e-to-the-x is e-to-the-x itself. And two inverse-trig ones: one over one-plus-x-squared integrates to arctan x; one over root one-minus-x-squared integrates to arcsin x. Always plus C.

The plus-C trap. On the left, someone computed the integral of three-x-squared plus four-x minus one and got x-cubed plus two-x-squared minus x. The arithmetic is right. The answer is incomplete. Without plus-C you've named one antiderivative, but the integral asks for all of them. On the AP rubric, that's a free point you just gave away. Every indefinite integral ends with plus C. No exceptions.

One nuance about plus-C and definite integrals. For a definite integral from a to b, we evaluate big-F at b minus big-F at a. Any plus-C would appear in both terms and cancel out. So you only need plus-C for indefinite integrals. For definite ones, leave it off — it's just noise. Example: the integral from zero to one of x-squared dx is one-third minus zero, which is one-third.

Why u-substitution exists. The chain rule says: the derivative of x-squared-plus-one to the fourth is four times x-squared-plus-one cubed, times two-x. The inside is x-squared-plus-one. Its derivative is two-x. The chain rule glued them together. So if an integrand has an inside function and a leftover factor that looks like its derivative, that's the chain rule's footprint. U-sub is how we walk it back.

The five-step recipe. One — pick u, usually the inside of a composite. Two — compute du by differentiating u with respect to x and multiplying by dx. Three — substitute every x-expression in the integrand, including the dx. Four — antidifferentiate the cleaner u-integral. Five — substitute x back. The formal statement is: the integral of f-of-g-of-x times g-prime-of-x dx equals the integral of f-of-u du. The messy chain unwinds.

Watch it work. The integral of two-x times cosine of x-squared dx. Let u equal x-squared. Then du is two-x dx. Look — the two-x dx in the original IS du, exactly. Substitute: now we have the integral of cosine of u du. That's just sine of u plus C. Last step — substitute x-squared back in for u. Answer: sine of x-squared plus C.

Sometimes du is off by a constant — fix it with a factor. The integral of x times x-squared-plus-one cubed dx. Let u equal x-squared-plus-one. Then du is two-x dx. But we only have x dx — that's half of du. So x dx equals one-half du. Pull the one-half out front. The integral becomes one-half times the integral of u-cubed du, which is one-half times u-to-the-fourth over four, plus C. Simplify: x-squared-plus-one to the fourth, over eight, plus C.
Your turn. Find the integral of x-squared times the square root of x-cubed-plus-five, dx. Pick u as the inside of the square root. Pause the video. Solve. Press play when you're ready.

Walking through it. Let u equal x-cubed plus five. Then du is three-x-squared dx. We have x-squared dx in our integrand — that's one-third of du. Pull one-third out front. The integral becomes one-third times the integral of root-u du. Root-u is u-to-the-one-half, so its antiderivative is u-to-the-three-halves over three-halves, which is two-thirds u-to-the-three-halves. Multiply by the one-third out front. Result: two-ninths times x-cubed-plus-five, to the three-halves power, plus C.

For definite integrals, the bounds change too. The integral from zero to one of two-x times x-squared-plus-one cubed dx. Let u equal x-squared-plus-one. When x is zero, u is one. When x is one, u is two. Don't skip this step — it's the second-most-common substitution mistake on the AP exam. New integral: from u equals one to u equals two, of u-cubed du. That's u-to-the-fourth over four. Evaluate: sixteen minus one, all over four — fifteen-quarters.

When the top degree is at least the bottom degree, divide first. Integral of x-squared-plus-one over x-minus-one, dx. Long division: x-squared-plus-one equals x-minus-one times x-plus-one, plus a remainder of two. So the integrand rewrites as x-plus-one, plus two over x-minus-one. Integrate term by term. You get x-squared over two, plus x, plus two times natural log of absolute value of x-minus-one, plus C.

Completing the square unlocks arctan. Integral of one over x-squared plus two-x plus five, dx. Complete the square on the bottom: x-squared plus two-x plus five equals x-plus-one quantity squared, plus four. So the denominator is something-squared plus four. Substitute u equals x-plus-one over two — then dx equals two du, and the denominator becomes four u-squared plus four, or four times u-squared-plus-one. The fours combine, leaving one-half times the integral of one over u-squared-plus-one du. That's one-half times arctan of x-plus-one over two, plus C.

FUN 6.14 — selecting techniques. Look at the integrand. Ask these four questions in order. One — does it match a basic rule from the table? Use it. Two — is there an inside function with its derivative sitting nearby? U-substitution. Three — is it a rational function where the top degree is at least the bottom? Long division first. Four — quadratic in the denominator with no real roots? Complete the square — that's almost always an arctan. Knowing which tool to pick is half the AP battle.

Recap. An antiderivative big-F means F-prime equals f. Always plus-C on indefinite integrals, never on definite. Reverse power rule, with one over x going to natural-log absolute-value. U-substitution is chain rule played backward — pick the inside, compute du, substitute everything including the dx. For definite integrals, change the bounds. Long division for top-heavy rational functions. Complete the square for irreducible quadratic denominators. And FUN 6.14 — ask the four questions in order.

How to actually master this module. One — read OpenStax Calculus Volume One, sections five-point-five through five-point-seven. Two — work the Khan Academy AP Calc AB Unit Six set on antiderivatives and substitution. Three — fifteen mixed problems in your dashboard. Four — book an advisor check-in if u-substitution bound changes still feel fuzzy. Next up: Module Nine — differential equations. Antiderivatives are the engine. Differential equations are where they earn their keep.
